model.label: Claude Sonnet 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Fast flagship Claude model for coding, analysis, and high-throughput agent use.

pick.rationale: Louisville enters this Round of 32 matchup as the analytically superior team despite being the lower seed, ranking #18 by KenPom (AdjEM +25.42) and #16 by Torvik with an elite two-way profile — a top-20 offense (AdjOE 124.1) and top-23 defense (AdjDE 98.7) — that meaningfully outpaces Michigan State's efficiency numbers (ORtg 117.3, 49th nationally; DRtg 101.7, 58th nationally). The Spartans are further undermined by the season-ending broken foot suffered by starting guard Divine Ugochukwu, a significant personnel loss that depletes MSU's backcourt depth heading into single-elimination play. Louisville's ACC battle-testing, superior adjusted efficiency on both ends of the floor, and full-strength roster give them a credible edge over a Michigan State team that, while dangerous under Tom Izzo, is operating shorthanded and ranked below the Cardinals in every major efficiency metric. The 3-vs-6 seed differential overstates MSU's advantage in this specific matchup, and Louisville's superior profile makes them the right side despite the inherent volatility of a Round of 32 game.

reasoningStep.title: Louisville vs. Michigan State — Round of 32 East Region Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Louisville holds a meaningful edge in adjusted efficiency metrics over Michigan State, with KenPom #18 vs. an implied MSU ranking in the mid-to-upper 20s, and a superior two-way profile (top-20 offense, top-23 defense vs. MSU's 49th/58th nationally). Michigan State's season-ending loss of Divine Ugochukwu to a broken foot is a significant injury blow to their backcourt. Louisville arrives at full strength with Mikel Brown Jr. returning, and their ACC schedule-strength validates their efficiency numbers against elite competition. Despite MSU's 3-seed and Tom Izzo's tournament pedigree, the efficiency gap and injury context favor Louisville.
evidence: Louisville KenPom #18, AdjEM +25.42, AdjOE 124.1 (20th nationally), AdjDE 98.7 (23rd nationally); Torvik #16 with matching elite two-way profile.
Michigan State 2025-26: 25-7 record, 2nd in Big Ten (15-5), SRS 23.44 (10th nationally), SOS 8th nationally, ORtg 117.3 (49th), DRtg 101.7 (58th) per Sports-Reference.
Divine Ugochukwu confirmed out for season with broken foot (season-ending surgery announced by Tom Izzo), depleting MSU's backcourt depth.
Louisville opened as 6.5-point favorite in Round of 64 vs. South Florida; Mikel Brown Jr. returned at full strength, validating their two-way efficiency profile.
Michigan State lost to UCLA 88-84 in Big Ten Tournament quarterfinals, ending their conference title hopes before the NCAA Tournament.
Jay Bilas picked Michigan State for a deep run, indicating expert respect for Izzo's program, but Louisville's efficiency advantage and MSU's injury context tip the balance to the Cardinals.

result.text: Michigan State March Madness bracket predictions for 2026 NCAA tournament Skip to main content Home PGA Tour LPGA College Amateur Golf Life Equipment Apparel Newsletter Videos Events Odds Betting USA TODAY Sports Contact Us Where Michigan State basketball stands in March Madness bracket predictions Christian Romo Detroit Free Press Show Caption Hide Caption MSU basketball video analysis on MSU’s Big Ten tournament loss to UCLA Lansing State Journal columnist Graham Couch and Detroit Free Press beat writer Chris Solari discuss MSU’s 88-84 loss to UCLA in the Big Ten tourney. Michigan State basketball 's Big Ten season is done. The question for the Spartans now is whether they will enter the 2026 NCAA Tournament with a No. 2 or a No. 3 seed. The 3-seed Spartans fell 88-84 to the UCLA Bruins in the quarterfinal of the 2026 Big Ten Tournament on Friday, March 13, ending their hopes of finishing the season with a conference title. And on Sunday, they will find out whether that loss was enough to knock them down a seed in the upcoming dance. We won't find out for sure until Sunday afternoon , but bracketology experts seem to be in agreement that Michigan State will retain its position as a No. 2 seed in March Madness, though there's a few that bumped MSU to a 3-seed Sunday morning. Watch NCAA basketball selection show on Fubo Helping Michigan State's chances are losses from other high-profile teams in their respective conference tournaments, including losses from 2-seed Nebraska and 4-seed Illinois in the Big Ten Tournament. Here is what the experts say before we learn how it all shakes out. SOLARI: Fast Big Ten tourney exit exposes MSU's thin margin for March mistakes Michigan State basketball bracketology for 2026 NCAA Tournament NET rankings: No. 11 (Last week: No. 11) All rankings entering Saturday's games. Quad 1: 9-6. Quad 2: 5-1. Quad 3: 7-0. Quad 4: 4-0. KenPom rankings: No. 10 (began last week No. 9) All rankings through Saturday's games. Offensive efficiency: No. 24. Defensive efficiency: No. 13. Michigan State basketball bracketology projections USA TODAY : 3-seed in South vs. Troy (updated March 15). ESPN : 2-seed in West vs. Sienna (updated March 15). CBS : 3-seed in East vs. North Dakota State (updated March 15). Fox Sports : 2-seed in West vs. Sienna (updated March 14). BracketMatrix.com : 2-seed (updated March 15). On3 : 2-seed in West vs. Furman (updated March 13). MSU dropped to a 3-seed in USA TODAY Sports' final projection , bypassed by Iowa State, which claims a 2-seed. Michigan State basketball results last week March 13: 88-84 loss vs UCLA – The Spartans ran into a red hot Donovan Dent, UCLA's star point guard, who finished with 23 points, 12 assists, six rebounds and four steals. The loss knocked Michigan State out of the Big Ten Tournament, preventing them from winning a conference title this season. (1:29) Myron Medcalf Mar 15, 2026, 06:45 PM ET Close Covers college basketball Joined ESPN.com in 2011 Graduate of Minnesota State University, Mankato Follow on X Multiple Authors Email Print Open Extended Reactions Get your popcorn ready because this NCAA tournament could be one of the best in recent memory. The top seeds are elite programs with the potential to cut down the nets. The underdogs are dangerous, too. The field also has undeniable star power. Top transfers such as Yaxel Lendeborg at Michigan and freshman stars (see: AJ Dybantsa , a candidate for the No. 1 pick in the 2026 NBA draft) are all in the same tier of teams this year, chasing the same dreams. It's a lot to consider. But we're here to help. Our annual NCAA tournament guide gives you the information you need to know about each team, pertinent information about any current issues that could impact their postseason, key injuries to note -- and, finally, our projections on their ceiling. We're got enough to help the nerds and the newcomers to the NCAA tournament chaos make sense of everything. Here is what you need to know about all 68 teams in the NCAA tournament. modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

result.text: Michigan State March Madness bracket predictions for 2026 NCAA tournament Skip to main content Home PGA Tour LPGA College Amateur Golf Life Equipment Apparel Newsletter Videos Events Odds Betting USA TODAY Sports Contact Us Where Michigan State basketball stands in March Madness bracket predictions Christian Romo Detroit Free Press Show Caption Hide Caption MSU basketball video analysis on MSU’s Big Ten tournament loss to UCLA Lansing State Journal columnist Graham Couch and Detroit Free Press beat writer Chris Solari discuss MSU’s 88-84 loss to UCLA in the Big Ten tourney. Michigan State basketball 's Big Ten season is done. The question for the Spartans now is whether they will enter the 2026 NCAA Tournament with a No. 2 or a No. 3 seed. The 3-seed Spartans fell 88-84 to the UCLA Bruins in the quarterfinal of the 2026 Big Ten Tournament on Friday, March 13, ending their hopes of finishing the season with a conference title. And on Sunday, they will find out whether that loss was enough to knock them down a seed in the upcoming dance. We won't find out for sure until Sunday afternoon , but bracketology experts seem to be in agreement that Michigan State will retain its position as a No. 2 seed in March Madness, though there's a few that bumped MSU to a 3-seed Sunday morning. Watch NCAA basketball selection show on Fubo Helping Michigan State's chances are losses from other high-profile teams in their respective conference tournaments, including losses from 2-seed Nebraska and 4-seed Illinois in the Big Ten Tournament. Here is what the experts say before we learn how it all shakes out. SOLARI: Fast Big Ten tourney exit exposes MSU's thin margin for March mistakes Michigan State basketball bracketology for 2026 NCAA Tournament NET rankings: No. 11 (Last week: No. 11) All rankings entering Saturday's games. Quad 1: 9-6. Quad 2: 5-1. Quad 3: 7-0. Quad 4: 4-0. KenPom rankings: No. 10 (began last week No. 9) All rankings through Saturday's games. Offensive efficiency: No. 24. Defensive efficiency: No. 13. Michigan State basketball bracketology projections USA TODAY : 3-seed in South vs. Troy (updated March 15). ESPN : 2-seed in West vs. Sienna (updated March 15). CBS : 3-seed in East vs. North Dakota State (updated March 15). Fox Sports : 2-seed in West vs. Sienna (updated March 14). BracketMatrix.com : 2-seed (updated March 15). On3 : 2-seed in West vs. Furman (updated March 13). MSU dropped to a 3-seed in USA TODAY Sports' final projection , bypassed by Iowa State, which claims a 2-seed. Michigan State basketball results last week March 13: 88-84 loss vs UCLA – The Spartans ran into a red hot Donovan Dent, UCLA's star point guard, who finished with 23 points, 12 assists, six rebounds and four steals. The loss knocked Michigan State out of the Big Ten Tournament, preventing them from winning a conference title this season. (1:29) Myron Medcalf Mar 15, 2026, 06:45 PM ET Close Covers college basketball Joined ESPN.com in 2011 Graduate of Minnesota State University, Mankato Follow on X Multiple Authors Email Print Open Extended Reactions Get your popcorn ready because this NCAA tournament could be one of the best in recent memory. The top seeds are elite programs with the potential to cut down the nets. The underdogs are dangerous, too. The field also has undeniable star power. Top transfers such as Yaxel Lendeborg at Michigan and freshman stars (see: AJ Dybantsa , a candidate for the No. 1 pick in the 2026 NBA draft) are all in the same tier of teams this year, chasing the same dreams. It's a lot to consider. But we're here to help. Our annual NCAA tournament guide gives you the information you need to know about each team, pertinent information about any current issues that could impact their postseason, key injuries to note -- and, finally, our projections on their ceiling. We're got enough to help the nerds and the newcomers to the NCAA tournament chaos make sense of everything. Here is what you need to know about all 68 teams in the NCAA tournament. modelTrace.content: {"pick":{"gameId":"r32-east-3","winnerId":"louisville","confidence":0.64,"rationale":"Louisville enters this Round of 32 matchup as the analytically superior team despite being the lower seed, ranking #18 by KenPom (AdjEM +25.42) and #16 by Torvik with an elite two-way profile — a top-20 offense (AdjOE 124.1) and top-23 defense (AdjDE 98.7) — that meaningfully outpaces Michigan State's efficiency numbers (ORtg 117.3, 49th nationally; DRtg 101.7, 58th nationally). The Spartans are further undermined by the season-ending broken foot suffered by starting guard Divine Ugochukwu, a significant personnel loss that depletes MSU's backcourt depth heading into single-elimination play. Louisville's ACC battle-testing, superior adjusted efficiency on both ends of the floor, and full-strength roster give them a credible edge over a Michigan State team that, while dangerous under Tom Izzo, is operating shorthanded and ranked below the Cardinals in every major efficiency metric. The 3-vs-6 seed differential overstates MSU's advantage in this specific matchup, and Louisville's superior profile makes them the right side despite the inherent volatility of a Round of 32 game."},"reasoningStep":{"id":"r32-east-3-analysis","title":"Louisville vs. Michigan State — Round of 32 East Region Analysis","summary":"Louisville holds a meaningful edge in adjusted efficiency metrics over Michigan State, with KenPom #18 vs. an implied MSU ranking in the mid-to-upper 20s, and a superior two-way profile (top-20 offense, top-23 defense vs. MSU's 49th/58th nationally). Michigan State's season-ending loss of Divine Ugochukwu to a broken foot is a significant injury blow to their backcourt.
